name: Lemon Grass Tea
description: Lemongrass flavored tea
ingredients: 200g lemon grass|1 litre water|250 ml milk|sugar
instructions: Clean the lemongrass thoroughly. Boil water in a pot. Tie the lemongrass into a bundle and dip it into the boiling water. Let it boil for 1 minute. Remove the lemongrass bundle or sieve it out. Add milk and sugar to taste. Remove from heat and serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Snack

name: African Basil Tea
description: Herbal tea flavored with Mujaja leaves
ingredients: 200g mujaja leaves|1 litre water|250ml milk|sugar
instructions: Pick and clean fresh Mujaja leaves with water. Boil water in a pot. Dip the clean Mujaja leaves into the boiling water. Boil for 1 minute. Remove from fire. Sieve to remove the leaves. Add milk and sugar to taste. Serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve with snacks like roasted groundnuts
alternative_cooking: Alternatively add desired sugar, Can add milk and tea leaves

name: Steamed Yams
description: Steamed yams cooked with skin on
ingredients: 4 medium sized Yams|water
instructions: Wash the yams thoroughly to remove soil. Place yams in a steamer pot with water. Steam for 2 and 1/2 hours until tender. Remove from heat. Serve with tea and porridge.

name: Matooke
description: Traditional Baganda steamed green bananas
ingredients: Green bananas|Banana leaves|Water
instructions: Peel bananas. Fasten them in fresh banana leaves using banana fibres to make omuwumbo. Put the muwumbo in a big saucepan having banana leaves stalks (emizingonyo) and moderate water. Add more banana leaves to cover the muwumbo properly. Put the contents of the saucepan on fire for 1 hour. When ready, press to check softness.
serving_suggestion: Serve with groundnut sauce, meat stew, chicken stew, bean sauce, fish, pea sauces, and greens like Dodo, Nakati, Ebbuga, Ejobyo and Ensugga.
alternative_cooking: Matooke can also be prepared in Katogo form: get peeled bananas or whole fingers and boil them wholly. Then fry either offal, beans, or meat with onions and tomatoes, then mix together with boiled matooke and serve.

name: Steamed Cassava
description: Steamed cassava
ingredients: Cassava|Banana leaves|Water
instructions: Peel cassava. Clean with water. Wrap in banana leaves and cook for 30-45 minutes.
serving_suggestion: Serve with tea, matooke, groundnut sauce, bean sauce, or meat stew.
alternative_cooking: Cassava can also be prepared in Katogo form.

name: Steamed Sweet Potatoes
description: Steamed sweet potatoes
ingredients: Sweet potatoes|Banana leaves|Water
instructions: Peel sweet potatoes. Clean with water. Wrap in banana leaves and cook for one hour.
serving_suggestion: Serve with tea, matooke, groundnut sauce, bean sauce, or meat stew.

name: Steamed Pumpkin
description: Steamed pumpkin
ingredients: Pumpkin|Banana leaves|Water
instructions: Divide pumpkin into medium sizes. Wrap in banana leaves and cook for one hour.
serving_suggestion: Serve with tea, matooke, groundnut sauce, bean sauce, or meat stew.

name: Fried Irish Potatoes
description: Fried Irish potatoes with tomatoes and onions
ingredients: Irish potatoes|Tomatoes|Onions|Salt|Water
instructions: Peel the potatoes and wash them. Fry onion and tomatoes in cooking oil. Add the potatoes and salt. Mix with the tomatoes and onions for 5-10 minutes. Add water. Cook until they are ready or soft, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with any sauce.

name: Groundnut Sauce
description: Groundnut sauce
ingredients: Groundnut flour or paste|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Grind the groundnuts. Mix with moderate water. Add onions. Cook on fire until the sauce thickens and oil separates.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.
alternative_cooking: Groundnuts with onions can also be put in a metallic saucepan with a fitting lid (box) and steamed together with the matooke. Groundnuts can also be prepared in Luwombo form: wrap in banana leaves, cover with banana leaves in a saucepan, and steam for 1 hour.

name: Meat Stew
description: Meat stew
ingredients: Meat|Tomatoes|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Place meat in a saucepan. Add salt. Put on fire and fry with its own fat. Add onion and tomatoes. Continue frying. When tending to dry, add enough water for sauce. Leave to boil until ready (1-2 hours), then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.
alternative_cooking: Meat can also be put in a metallic saucepan with a fitting lid (box) with salt, onions, and tomatoes, then steamed together with the matooke. Meat can also be prepared in Luwombo form: wrap in banana leaves, cover with banana leaves in a saucepan, and steam for 1 hour.

name: Chicken Stew
description: Chicken stew
ingredients: Chicken|Tomatoes|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Cut chicken into medium sized pieces. Place chicken in a saucepan. Add salt. Put on fire and fry with its own fat. Add onion and tomatoes. Continue frying. When tending to dry, add enough water for sauce. Leave to boil until ready, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.
alternative_cooking: Chicken can also be put in a metallic saucepan with a fitting lid (box) with salt, onions, and tomatoes, then steamed together with the matooke. Chicken can also be prepared in Luwombo form: wrap in banana leaves, cover with banana leaves in a saucepan, and steam for 1 hour.

name: Bean Stew
description: Bean stew
ingredients: Beans|Tomatoes|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Get fresh beans. Clean by sorting first, then wash with water. Boil them with salt until ready or soft. Drain out the soup. Heat cooking oil in another pan. Add onions and tomatoes. When ready, add beans, then the drained soup. Boil for 15 minutes, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.

name: Fish Stew
description: Fish stew
ingredients: Fish|Tomatoes|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Get fish. Heat cooking oil in a pan. Add onions and tomatoes. When ready, add fish. Add water and salt. Cook until ready, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.

name: Pea Stew
description: Pea stew
ingredients: Peas|Tomatoes|Water|Salt|Onions
instructions: Get fresh peas. Clean by sorting first, then wash with water. Boil them with salt until ready or soft. Drain out the soup. Heat cooking oil in another pan. Add onions and tomatoes. When ready, add peas, then the drained soup. Boil for 15 minutes, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, cassava, or sweet potatoes.

name: Traditional Sorghum Beer
description: Traditional sorghum-based alcoholic drink
ingredients: Sorghum|Honey|Water
instructions: Sorghum in a sack is dipped in water, such as in a river, for about 24 hours. The sorghum may or may not be mixed with ash. If it is mixed with ash, use half a basin of ash for 1 sack of sorghum. Mixing in ash solution ensures that the sorghum does not change colour from red to black. Pour the mixture on the floor in the house or outside and leave it for about 4 days. Germination continues. Take outside for drying on mats, carpets, or polythene paper. Some of the ash is left on the ground. Drying takes 1-3 days depending on the weather. When levels of humidity are high, drying takes 5 days. Keep turning it. Growth stops. Gather and clean the dirt by winnowing, then transfer it to a miller to grind it to flour. Use local stones to grind. Warm some water to 60°C for about 5 minutes. Add flour to water and stir until you obtain a thick but homogeneous mixture. Do not let it boil. Remove it from the fire after 5 minutes. It is now like Akaunga. Boil more water separately, an amount equivalent to half of the porridge. Leave it to cool. If mixed while hot, it spoils the porridge. Mix the cold water with the porridge and stir. One can drink this cold as porridge. Let this stay in a pan, pot, or bucket. Take half of the porridge after 5 hours and mix with honey (1 cup of honey for every 5 litres of porridge). Mix with the rest of the porridge. Leave the porridge for 4-5 days covered with a lid. After 2-3 days, it is sweet and can be drunk by those who like it sweet. After 5-6 days, it will be an alcoholic drink called Enturire. This method produces a rather sweet alcohol but needs about 2 weeks to prepare.
alternative_cooking: Another method of preparing Enturire: After mixing the cold water with the porridge and stirring in the previous method, leave the porridge for 5 days without adding honey. Then put honey and cover immediately and let it stay covered for a day. The cover can be a lid or sack. After this, you get Enturire. When preparing Enturire using this method, the Enturire is very strong and one cup can easily knock one out. To prepare Omulamba: Instead of adding honey to the porridge as in Enturire, add to the porridge twice its volume of water and stir. Leave the porridge covered for 6-7 days. It becomes a diluted alcoholic drink. Boil this porridge for about 2 hours to get Omusaze or Omutekye. If you keep Omusaze for 3-4 days it forms froth. Add germinated sorghum flour to thicken and ferment Omusaze and also to prevent further formation of froth. This is now called Omushemburo. Omushemburo is thicker. Boil more water and put it in germinated sorghum flour. Make porridge to the same temperature as Enturire (60°C). Remove it and cool. Mix it with Omushemburo. Leave it overnight and what you will have in the morning is Omulamba.

name: Amaranthus with Groundnut Sauce
description: Amaranthus leaves with groundnut sauce
ingredients: 2 bunches Amaranthus leaves|Groundnut flour|Onions|Tomatoes|Green pepper|Carrots|Curry powder|African eggplant
ingredient_names: amaranthus leaves|groundnut flour|onions|tomatoes|green pepper|carrots|curry powder|african eggplant
instructions: Clean the amaranthus. Chop it and put it into a pot. Boil it in half a cup of water until it cooks (about 10 minutes). Add 4 spoons (2/3 of a glass) of groundnut flour. Let it boil and stir. Cook it briefly (about 5 minutes) and remove it from the fire.
serving_suggestion: Serve with sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes, rice, posho, or matoke.
alternative_cooking: Once cooked with groundnuts, you can add onions, tomatoes, and flavours like green pepper, carrots, African eggplant (Entura), and curry powder.

name: Eshwiga with Beans
description: Eswiga leaves cooked with beans
ingredients: Eswiga leaves|Beans|Onions|Salt|Tomatoes
instructions: Clean the eswiga and chop it (optional). Take cooked beans and mix with eswiga. Cook the two together. Add salt to taste and serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with posho (Obuhunga), sweet potatoes (Ebiribwa), Irish potatoes (Emondi), Engano (wheat made like posho), or millet bread.
alternative_cooking: Method 1: Chop the vegetables. Get a clean saucepan, add oil and heat. Add onions, then tomatoes, and after this add eswiga. Cook for about 30 minutes or until it tastes cooked. Method 2: Chop off the hard stems of eswiga and clean. Put above cooking beans, Irish potatoes, or sweet potatoes for 15 minutes. Once ready, separate from main food without mixing. Add salt and serve as a side dish for other meals like beans or potatoes. Once cooked, you can also fry with oil and onions (normally without tomatoes) and serve. Method 3: Once beans and vegetables are ready, you may mix to make a sauce (not fried) and cook briefly. You can add onions and tomatoes and cook together.

name: Irish Potatoes with Vegetables
description: Irish potatoes with vegetables and beans
ingredients: Irish potatoes|Green vegetables|Tomatoes|Onions|Carrots|Groundnuts|Beans|Salt|Water
instructions: Peel the potatoes and wash them. Boil them for about 20 minutes. At this point, you can add the green vegetables. Add cooked beans and salt to taste, then serve.
alternative_cooking: Once the potatoes are cooked, you can add tomatoes, onions, carrots, groundnuts (for those with money), and green vegetables, then serve.

name: Fermented Sorghum Drink
description: Fermented sorghum drink
ingredients: Sorghum flour|Water
instructions: Add 3 litres of cold water to 10 kilos of sorghum flour. Pour it into a drum of hot water. Mix and wait for two hours. Then add about 20 litres of cold but boiled water and mix. Wait for 1-2 days. It is now ready to drink.

name: Traditional Sorghum Bread
description: Traditional sorghum bread
ingredients: Sorghum flour|Water
instructions: Pick sorghum (omukoma). Then thresh, winnow, and clean. Then pass over fire on a frying pan so that it assumes a very good taste. Then grind it. Boil water. Add the sorghum flour and mix. When ready, then serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with meat, beans mixed with little ghee or butter (amajuta gente), peas, greens (eshwiga, ekicuringanyi), or slippery sauce like enderema and ekituruguma.

name: Cabbage with Beans
description: Traditional cabbage with beans or peas
ingredients: Beans or peas|Salt|Cabbage
instructions: Chop the cabbage into small pieces. Clean by washing with water. Boil with cooked peas or beans.
serving_suggestion: Serve with obuhemba, sweet potatoes (ebiribwa), or smashed Irish potatoes.

name: Katogo
description: A one-pot dish combining bananas with sauce
ingredients: 20 fingers green bananas|1 mug water|1 onion|2 small tomatoes|1/4 teaspoon salt|1/4 kg meat or intestines or beans|Cooking oil
instructions: Pick bananas. Peel the banana fingers. Clean remains of sap with water (only when dirty). Leave to drain if cleaned with water. Heat oil in pan. Put onions. Add tomatoes. Add the peeled banana fingers. Fry for a few minutes. Add water and salt. Leave to cook for 15-20 minutes on high fire.
alternative_cooking: Meat is fried while intestines are pre-boiled for half an hour (no water is added). To prepare: chop meat into small pieces. Heat oil. Add onion when golden brown. Add tomatoes. Add meat or pre-boiled intestines. Cook until ready for about 15-30 minutes. Serve with Katogo as above.

name: Chicken Chaps
description: A chicken dish from Mbale
ingredients: 1 whole chicken|2 eggs|200g wheat flour|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt|500ml cooking oil
instructions: Cut chicken into pieces. Boil the chicken for 15 minutes. Strain out the water and let it dry. Chop onion into fine pieces. Mix eggs with wheat flour and chopped onions. Add salt to taste. Smear the mixture onto the chicken pieces. Deep fry until golden brown (about 3 minutes).
serving_suggestion: Serve with matoke and stew or soup.

name: Lime Water
description: Lime water taken for cough, weight loss, diabetes or high blood pressure
ingredients: 2 limes|500ml water|1 teaspoon sugar (optional)
instructions: Cut limes into two halves. Squeeze each half to release the juice. Squeeze directly into clean water. Mix by stirring. Add sugar to taste (optional).
serving_suggestion: Serve with snacks such as bread, biscuits, or other foods.

name: Groundnut Flour Sauce
description: Groundnut flour sauce
ingredients: 500g groundnut flour|1 litre water|2 tomatoes|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Take groundnut flour (0.5 kg). Mix with cold water and put on fire. Let it boil as it forms foam. Wait until the foam has stopped. Add tomatoes and onions. Reduce the fire and let it simmer. Cook for one hour. Remove from the fire. Add salt to taste.
serving_suggestion: Serve with sweet potatoes.
alternative_cooking: As Luwombo: mix flour with cold water, add onion and salt. Get a young banana leaf and heat it shortly, then curve it and pour the mixture. Tie and steam for 1 to 1.5 hours.

name: Cowpea Seed Sauce
description: Cowpea seed sauce
ingredients: 500g cowpea seeds|2 litres water|1 pinch magadi or oluvu|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Grind the cowpea seeds to remove the outer cover and break them up. Winnow on a tray to get rid of the covers. Put water in a pan. Add a pinch of oluvu or magadi to boil the water. Add the ground seeds. Then add two cups of water. Let it boil for one hour. Remove extra covers that float on boiling water using a spoon as you boil. Add salt as the food cools.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke.
alternative_cooking: Boil peas then mash. Fry onions and tomatoes. Add mashed peas and water, then boil and serve.

name: Cassava with Beans
description: Cassava with beans
ingredients: 500g cassava|200g beans|1 onion|2 tomatoes|500ml water
instructions: Peel the cassava and remove the pith. Wash. Cut the cassava into medium sized pieces. Get a saucepan and add the chopped cassava. Put the already cooked beans on top of the cassava. Fry onions and tomatoes separately, then add on top of the beans. Add water. Cover the saucepan with a plate or lid (banana leaves are not preferred). Cook for 30 minutes on mild fire. Open up spaces using a wooden spoon to let beans mix with cassava. Remove from the fire. Do not mash or mix the beans with cassava.
alternative_cooking: Serve with tea or any beverage. (Note: This is a main dish that can be eaten as is.)

name: Smoked Fish with Groundnut Sauce
description: Smoked fish with groundnut sauce
ingredients: 300g smoked or dried fish|500ml water|2 tomatoes|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt|200g groundnut flour
instructions: Get smoked or dried roasted fish and clean it. Break it into small pieces. Put water in a pan. Put on fire. Add tomatoes, onion, and salt. Cover and let it cook for 30 minutes. Mix in the groundnut flour. Add the fish and let it cook for a further 30 minutes (the fish is dry so it takes long to cook).
serving_suggestion: Serve with muwogo, kaunga, karo, banana, or sweet potato.
alternative_cooking: Can also be prepared in Luwombo form.

name: White Ants Dish
description: Termite-based dish with high protein and fats
ingredients: 2 kg white termites|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Obtain 2 kg of dry white termites either from storage or market. Put in a saucepan. Dry fry for 5-10 minutes on strong fire. Add salt to taste. Remove from heat. Pound termites to flour in a mortar and grind to paste. In Gulu, a special grinding stone (Kidi-rego) is used. Repeat grinding up to 3 times until the product is a paste.
serving_suggestion: Serve with cooked cassava (gwana), sweet potatoes (Laiyata), or yams (Obaro).
alternative_cooking: Ngwen acela (Dry fried ants method 1): Get fresh white termites. Put a little water in a pot and heat. Put clean termites in pot and cover to generate enough steam to kill termites. Boil or steam for two minutes and remove from fire. Put termites in a saucepan and dry fry while mixing until dry. Add salt to taste. Put on a winnowing tray and remove legs and wings. Rub the termites briefly then pass a light current of air to blow away the wings. You may also remove the wings as you eat. This process is ideal for large amounts and served as a snack. Ngwen acela (Dry fried ants method 2): Dry fry fresh termites directly without boiling. Add salt to taste. Turn continuously. Eat as a snack while removing the wings. Dek ngwen (White ant sauce): Mix equal volumes of water and processed termites. Boil until mixture turns to white soup. Then fry with a little oil and onions and add tomatoes. Serve with kwon kal (Atapa), potatoes, or cassava. Dek ngwen with simsim or groundnut paste: Mix equal volumes of water and processed termites. Boil until mixture turns to white soup. Add tomatoes. When tomatoes are ready, remove from fire and add simsim paste. Serve with kwon kal (Atapa), potatoes, or cassava. Ajabu (White ant balls): Put freshly harvested termites in a bag. Shake to break off wings. Remove wings. Grind termites to a paste. Boil water in a pot. Gently add paste in portions or balls into the hot water. The pasty balls stick together into a ball. Repeat so that you have a number of balls in the water. Do not add any cold water as this will break the balls up into a soup. The balls are like meat in the soup. Serve with potatoes, cassava, or fresh pounded cassava (kwon but).

name: Akeyo Leaves
description: Akeyo vegetable dish
ingredients: 2 handfuls Akeyo leaves|2 litres water|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon traditional salt or soda ash|100g groundnut and simsim paste
instructions: Pluck the leaves together with leaf stalks and sometimes even the young shoots (those that can be easily plucked). Discard the old mature stalks. Clean vegetable well. Place the plucked leaves on a chopping board and cut them very small. Use 2 litres of water for 2 handfuls of the cut akeyo leaves (cooking for 2 persons). Add the cut leaves and the stalks to the water and leave to cook for about 30 minutes on high fire. Then add traditional salt or soda ash and let it stay on low fire for 30 minutes. By this time the water would have reduced considerably. Remove from fire. Drain the water that has remained and put it aside. Do not pour away because you will need it a little later. Add groundnut and simsim paste and mix while adding a little of the water you had earlier drained out and put aside. Akeyo should be a thick sauce and hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve with sweet potatoes or cassava.
alternative_cooking: Akeyo (traditional method): Clean vegetable. Cut into small pieces (just as you cut Dodo). Boil water. Put leaves. Cook on low fire for 1-2 hours or until it feels soft when squeezed between the fingers. Remove water completely. Add salt. Pour groundnut paste (a lot of groundnuts are needed). Mix. Serve with sweet potatoes or cassava. This method is NOT traditional. Fried Akeyo: Fry onions in a pan with cooking oil. Add tomatoes and flavourings such as curry powder. Cook briefly. Add your boiled vegetables. Let cook briefly. Serve with cassava or sweet potatoes but not posho.

name: Malakwang
description: Sour vegetable dish from malakwang leaves
ingredients: Fresh Malakwang leaves|100g groundnut and simsim paste|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Remove mid veins from the leaves. Wash vegetables in cold water (never in hot water). Let it boil for 10 minutes while turning regularly to cook well. Remove from heat and drain the water into a bowl. Soak vegetables in fresh cold water to reduce the sour taste. Take a portion of the drained water and add groundnut or simsim paste or a mixture of the two. Mix thoroughly. Remove vegetables from cold water and mix with paste. If you need the vegetable more sour, add more of the drained water. If you need it less sour, replace with fresh water. Boil briefly. Add salt to taste.
serving_suggestion: Best served with sweet potatoes, cassava, and kwon kal (finger millet posho or ugali).
alternative_cooking: Drain only a portion of the water into a bowl. Mix part of the drained portion with groundnut or simsim paste or a mixture of both. If you need it more sour, add more of the drained water. If too sour, add fresh water. Boil briefly. Serve with sweet potatoes, cassava, or kwon kal.

name: Eboo
description: A traditional vegetable dish good for vitamin C
ingredients: 1 pinch magadi|500ml water|2 handfuls Eboo leaves|1 teaspoon salt|100g groundnut paste
instructions: Remove stalks to be left with leaves only. Dry for ten minutes in sun (optional). Wash. Boil water and add a pinch of magadi or abalang. Add vegetable and let simmer for ten minutes. Add salt to taste. Add groundnut paste (optional) and cook further for 5 minutes for the groundnuts.
serving_suggestion: Serve with posho (Euga), Atap, or Acok.
alternative_cooking: Instead of adding groundnuts, you can fry the vegetable: fry onion in hot oil. Add tomatoes. Add vegetable and cook briefly for 30 minutes.

name: Finger Millet Porridge with Tamarind
description: Good for iron and carbohydrates for all age groups
ingredients: 1 litre water|200g finger millet flour|100g tamarind or 250ml milk
instructions: Boil 0.5 litre of water. Mix flour in cool 0.5 litre water and pour it into the boiling water. Stir. Cook for about fifteen minutes. Add tamarind extract or milk. To prepare tamarind extract: remove covers of ripe tamarind fruits. Use cold water to extract the pulp by pressing between fingers in water. Add the extract to the cooked porridge. Let it boil briefly.
serving_suggestion: Serve for breakfast. Also good for a woman who has just given birth to increase breast milk production.
alternative_cooking: For unripe tamarind fruit: put the unripe cleaned Epedur in hot water when intact (mbichi). Boil in water for two minutes. Remove from fire and let it cool. Squeeze to make extract (kamua). Remove fruit remains from juice (chuja). Use this to flavour your food, mainly uji (porridge) or atapa, especially inginyoit (sweet potato flour).

name: Smoked Fish
description: Tender smoked fish simmered in a rich tomato and onion stew until falling apart and flavorful.
ingredients: 500g smoked fish|3 tablespoons oil|1 onion|2 tomatoes|500ml water|1 teaspoon salt|100g groundnuts (optional)
instructions: Clean the dried fish with warm water. Heat oil, then add onions and tomatoes. Add the fish after the oil, onions, and tomatoes are ready. Add salt and any flavourings, then cover. Add enough water to cover the fish. Cook for 45 minutes to 1 hour for Aputa, Agaria, and Ekolea, or for 1 to 1.5 hours for Ebileng.
serving_suggestion: Serve with Atap, Emuwogo, Emuceri, or Acok.
alternative_cooking: Once fish is fried, you may add groundnuts and cook a bit more, but this is optional. When the fish is about to get ready, you may add fresh cabbage, eggplant, carrot, or Entula. To cook Akobokob with the above vegetables or fish, cook the cleaned fruit first with some magadi or Abalaga as it is very hard, then add to the other foods once cooked. As Ebileng is hard to cook, Akobokob can be cooked at the same time.

name: Dried Mushrooms
description: Earthy dried mushrooms harvested from termite mounds, rehydrated and cooked into a savory sauce that can be stored for up to a year.
ingredients: 500ml water|200g dried Eswei|1 pinch magadi or Abalang|1 teaspoon salt|100g groundnuts (optional)
instructions: Harvest Eswei from a termite mound. Remove the base part that has soil. Dry in the sun for 1-2 days until dry. Can be stored for a long time in this form. Soak in cold water for about half an hour. Boil water. Add a little magadi or Abalang. Put Eswei in the boiling water while rinsing. Cook for about 30 minutes or until soft. Add salt. Add groundnuts to thicken the sauce (optional) and boil briefly.
serving_suggestion: Serve with Atap, Acok, cassava (Emuwogo), or posho.

name: Steamed Matooke
description: Traditional Baganda steamed green bananas, carefully wrapped in banana leaves and slow-cooked until soft and ready for mashing.
ingredients: 10-15 green matooke fingers|Banana leaves|Banana leaf stalks|500ml water
instructions: Peel matooke and wash with plain clean water. Prepare the banana leaf stalks (ebikolokomba) proportional to the size of the saucepan so that they fit at the base (about 3-4 inches). Add enough water to the level of the stalks. Carefully prepare at least four banana leaves by removing the stalk (omuzingonyo). Place at least two well-prepared banana leaves in the basket in a crosswise manner, then add the matooke. Neatly arrange and wrap the matooke with 3 banana leaves and secure them with banana fibers (Ebyayi). Carry the well-packed matooke into the earlier prepared saucepan. One by one, tuck the banana leaves on top of each other until you are satisfied that they fit snugly. Place the matooke on moderate fire for at least 45 minutes to 1 hour. Regularly check the matooke to ensure it does not run out of water to avoid burning. When the banana leaves have turned brown, this indicates that the matooke is ready to be mashed. Remove the ready matooke from the saucepan and place them in a basket for mashing using a fresh banana leaf. Wrap the mashed matooke neatly in banana leaves and return to the saucepan, adding more water underneath. Put the matooke back on the fire and leave it to boil before reducing heat and simmering for at least another hour.
alternative_cooking: This is the full traditional method for preparing matooke for special occasions.

name: Braised Matooke (Katogo)
description: A hearty one-pot melange of green bananas simmered with vegetables until soft and richly flavored.
ingredients: 10 green matooke fingers|2 carrots|3 cloves garlic|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt|2 tablespoons ghee|1 bunch amaranth (dodo)|2 tablespoons tomato paste|5 garden eggs (entula) or bitter berries (katunkuma)|2 tomatoes
instructions: Put the matooke into a heavy saucepan and add water halfway, then bring to a boil. Do NOT add salt. Reduce the heat and let it simmer gently for at least an hour. Meanwhile, heat some vegetable oil in a medium saucepan and fry the garlic and onions, then add the rest of the vegetables including salt and pepper. Add a bit of water and simmer gently over low heat for forty-five minutes. Correct the seasoning. As soon as the matooke is done, drain the water and reserve some of it. Immediately after draining the matooke, add the vegetables and return your saucepan to the fire. Once it begins to boil, reduce the heat at once and cook for another half hour.
alternative_cooking: This meatless version of Katogo is rich with garden eggs and amaranth.

name: Steamed Plantain
description: Sweet and tender steamed ripe plantains, cooked in their skins to lock in moisture and natural sugars.
ingredients: 4 ripe plantains
instructions: Wash and cut off the tip and end of each plantain but do not peel. Arrange in the top of a double boiler and steam covered over low heat for about 45 minutes.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a side dish or breakfast.

name: Fried Plantain
description: Crispy and caramelized deep-fried ripe plantain slices, golden on the outside and soft inside.
ingredients: 4 ripe plantains|500ml cooking oil
instructions: Peel ripe plantains and slice into half lengthwise or chop into small slices. Deep fry in hot cooking oil until you get a nice golden color.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack, side dish, or breakfast.

name: Fried Cassava
description: Crispy deep-fried cassava strips, seasoned with aromat and pepper for a savory, crunchy snack or side dish.
ingredients: 500g cassava|1 teaspoon aromat|1 teaspoon pepper|500ml vegetable oil
instructions: Wash and peel the cassava, then cut into thin strips. Remove the membrane from the middle. Drain and wipe dry, then set aside. Heat the oil until it is almost smoking, then place a few cassava pieces in the oil and fry until they become brownish. Remove from the oil and sprinkle a liberal amount of aromat and pepper. Salt is a ready substitute.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack or side dish.

name: Fried Cassava Balls
description: Creamy mashed cassava rolled into golden balls, coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until crispy outside and soft inside.
ingredients: 500g cassava|500ml water|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon pepper|1 teaspoon onion powder|1 teaspoon garlic powder|100g bread crumbs|2 eggs|500ml vegetable oil
instructions: Wash, peel, and cut the cassava into cubes. Set aside. Boil the cassava with a little salt until it is cooked. Strain and set aside until it cools. When ready to fry, add the onion powder, garlic powder, and pepper. Mash well until everything is well mixed, then shape into large marble-sized balls. Mix in the beaten egg, then roll the cassava balls into the bread crumbs. Deep fry until they are a nice golden brown color.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack or appetizer.

name: Boiled Irish Potatoes
description: Soft, tender boiled potatoes rolled in melted butter, simply seasoned with salt for a comforting side dish.
ingredients: 500g potatoes|1 litre water|1 teaspoon salt|2 tablespoons butter
instructions: Bring the potatoes to a boil with some salt added to the saucepan. Reduce the heat and simmer gently for about 20 to 40 minutes. When they are tender, drain well and roll them in the butter or margarine.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a side dish with stews or vegetables.

name: Millet Bread
description: Traditional thick and hearty millet bread, slowly stirred to a smooth, lump-free consistency and served in sliced portions.
ingredients: 500g millet flour|1 litre water
instructions: Use a heavy pan that does not burn easily. Bring a couple of cups of water to a boil, then add about half of the millet. Stir the millet briskly using a wooden spatula to ensure that all the water becomes absorbed to the point where the bread is thick. Keep adding more flour and water as needed while you keep turning the millet, making sure there are no lumps. Use low heat. Make certain there is no raw flour and no lumps. When cooked, turn it onto a platter (a traditional serving basket is even better). Traditionally, millet bread is served sliced with a strong thread and cut into small portions suitable for serving guests.
serving_suggestion: Serve with groundnut sauce, meat stew, or greens.

name: Cassava and Banana Pancakes
description: Sweet and soft pancakes made from ripe bananas and cassava flour, fried until golden brown and caramelized on the edges.
ingredients: 200g cassava flour|4 ripe bananas|1 teaspoon vanilla essence|500ml cooking oil
instructions: Peel and mash the ripe bananas. While mashing, add some cassava flour and vanilla. Keep kneading and working the dough until it is stiff to the point where it is not sticky. Lay the dough on a floured board and roll to about an inch thickness. Using a cookie cutter or an ordinary glass, cut into rounds. Heat the oil in a heavy duty saucepan or frying pan. When the oil is hot, fry the Kabalagala until they are a nice brown hue. Drain and set aside.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack or breakfast with tea.

name: Sweet Sesame Balls
description: Sweet and nutty sesame seed balls bound with sugar syrup, rolled while warm into bite-sized treats.
ingredients: 200g simsim (sesame seeds)|5 tablespoons sugar|500ml water|1 pinch salt
instructions: Add 5 tablespoons of sugar to half a litre of water and bring to a boil until it forms a syrup. Add the simsim and bind well. Set aside. While still warm, grease the palms of your hands and roll the warm simsim into balls about an inch in diameter.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack or dessert.

name: Roasted Sesame Seeds
description: Nutty and aromatic roasted sesame seeds, gently toasted until golden brown and lightly salted.
ingredients: 200g simsim (sesame seeds)|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Wash the simsim and drain well, then add salt. Heat a medium sized frying pan over low heat and add the simsim. Watch for any signs of burning and constantly stir until the simsim turns brown.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack, topping for bread, or mixed with honey.

name: Bean Dish with Skin Removed
description: Smooth and creamy skinned beans slow-cooked with garden eggs, onions, garlic, and aromatic curry powder.
ingredients: 300g fresh beans|5 garden eggs (entula)|1 onion|3 cloves garlic|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon pepper|1 teaspoon curry powder|2 tablespoons cooking oil|1 tablespoon ghee
instructions: Soak the beans overnight and remove the skin. When ready, boil the beans until they are tender. When cooked, drain and set aside. Heat a little oil in a saucepan and fry the onions until they become translucent. Then add garlic, tomatoes, entula, and roasted curry powder. At this point, add the cooked beans and cook a while longer over low heat, stirring to avoid burning. If necessary, add some of the stock from the beans. Before serving, make sure that the garden eggs are cooked.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, sweet potatoes, or cassava.

name: Boiled Groundnuts
description: Soft and savory groundnuts boiled in their pods with salt until tender and creamy inside.
ingredients: 500g groundnuts (in pods)|2 litres water|2 tablespoons salt
instructions: Place the groundnuts in a pan. Add water and salt and bring to a boil. Cover and reduce heat. Cook for about 45 minutes or until cooked.
serving_suggestion: Serve in their pods as a snack.

name: Pan Fried Groundnuts
description: Crunchy salt-fried groundnuts roasted in a pan until the skins peel easily, perfect for snacking.
ingredients: 500g groundnuts|2 tablespoons salt|100ml water
instructions: Mix water and salt to make a solution. Set aside. Add the solution to the groundnuts and mix well. Use a heavy saucepan. Fry them over low heat, constantly stirring, until they peel easily.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack.

name: Nakati
description: Tender Nakati greens lightly boiled with vinegar, a simple and nutritious side dish.
ingredients: 300g Nakati leaves|500ml water|1 tablespoon vinegar|1 teaspoon salt
instructions: Wash the Nakati leaves thoroughly. Boil water in a saucepan. Add the Nakati leaves. Add vinegar and salt. Boil for 5-10 minutes until tender. Drain excess water and serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a side dish with matooke, sweet potatoes, or cassava.

name: Peas Stew
description: Rich and creamy dried pea stew slow-cooked for hours with onions and ghee until velvety smooth.
ingredients: 500g dry crushed peas|2 litres water|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon rock salt|2 tablespoons ghee
instructions: Soak the dry crushed peas overnight if possible. Place peas in a saucepan with water. Add onions, salt, and rock salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 2 to 3 hours until peas are soft and creamy. Stir occasionally and add more water if needed. Finish with ghee before serving.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, posho, or bread.

name: Fried Grasshoppers
description: Crispy and golden fried grasshoppers seasoned with onions and curry powder, a popular Ugandan delicacy.
ingredients: 300g grasshoppers|1 onion|1 teaspoon curry powder|3 tablespoons cooking oil
instructions: Use a medium size saucepan. Add the grasshoppers with a little oil, sliced onion, and roasted curry powder. Cook over low heat, turning frequently to ensure they do not burn. When ready to serve, they should have a nice golden brown colour and be somewhat crispy.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack.

name: Dressed Chicken Luwombo
description: Whole chicken grilled over banana peels then steamed in banana leaves with tomatoes, shallots, and potatoes in the traditional Luwombo style.
ingredients: 1 whole chicken|3 tomatoes|2 tablespoons tomato paste|4 cloves garlic|5 shallots|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon pepper|5 Luwombo leaves|3 Irish potatoes|Banana leaves|3 tablespoons cooking oil|2 tablespoons margarine|500ml chicken stock|1 onion
ingredient_names: chicken|tomatoes|tomato paste|garlic|shallots|salt|pepper|luwombo leaves|irish potatoes|banana leaves|cooking oil|margarine|chicken stock|onions
instructions: Have ready a charcoal stove that is alight and fiery hot. Spread banana peels over the embers. Grill the chicken whole until it becomes a nice golden brown, turning occasionally. When done, remove and set aside. Drop the tomatoes in boiling water for 1 minute, then peel and chop. In a bowl, combine chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, minced garlic, chopped shallots, salt, pepper, and margarine. Stuff the mixture inside the chicken and spread some on the outside. Place Luwombo leaves at the bottom of a banana leaf. Add the chicken, sliced Irish potatoes, and chicken stock. Wrap tightly with banana leaves and secure with banana fibers. Place the Luwombo package in a saucepan with water at the bottom. Cover and steam for 1.5 to 2 hours over low heat.
serving_suggestion: Serve with matooke, sweet potatoes, or rice.

name: Indian Chicken Curry
description: Classic Indian chicken curry with tender browned chicken simmered in a rich, aromatic yogurt and tomato sauce.
ingredients: 800g chicken breast|2 teaspoons salt|3 tablespoons cooking oil|1 onion|4 cloves garlic|1 tablespoon ginger|2 tablespoons curry powder|1 teaspoon cumin|1 teaspoon turmeric|1 teaspoon coriander|1 teaspoon cayenne pepper|1 tablespoon water|400g crushed tomatoes|1/2 cup yogurt|2 tablespoons cilantro|1 teaspoon garam masala|1 tablespoon lemon juice
ingredient_names: chicken breast|salt|cooking oil|onion|garlic|ginger|curry powder|cumin|turmeric|coriander|cayenne pepper|water|crushed tomatoes|yogurt|cilantro|garam masala|lemon juice
instructions: Sprinkle the chicken breasts with 2 teaspoons salt. Heat oil in a large skillet over high heat. Partially cook the chicken in the hot oil in batches until completely browned on all sides. Transfer browned chicken breasts to a plate and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium and add onion, garlic, and ginger to the oil remaining in the skillet. Cook and stir until onion turns soft and translucent, about 5 to 8 minutes. Stir curry powder, cumin, turmeric, coriander, cayenne, and 1 tablespoon of water into the onion mixture. Allow to heat together for about 1 minute while stirring. Add crushed tomatoes, yogurt, 1 tablespoon chopped cilantro, and 1 teaspoon salt to the mixture. Stir to combine. Return chicken breasts to the skillet along with any juices on the plate. Pour in 1/2 cup water and bring to a boil, turning the chicken to coat with the sauce. Sprinkle garam masala and 1 tablespoon cilantro over the chicken. Cover the skillet and simmer until chicken breasts are no longer pink in the center and the juices run clear, about 20 minutes. An instant-read thermometer inserted into the center should read at least 165 degrees F (74 degrees C). Drizzle with lemon juice to serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with rice, naan, or chapati.

name: Chicken Stir-Fry
description: Classic chicken stir-fry with crisp vegetables in a savory soy and brown sugar glaze, served over fluffy rice.
ingredients: 500ml water|250g white rice|100ml soy sauce|50g brown sugar|1 tablespoon cornstarch|1 tablespoon ginger|3 cloves garlic|1 teaspoon red pepper flakes|500g chicken breast|2 tablespoons sesame oil|200g broccoli|1 onion|2 carrots|150g water chestnuts|1 green bell pepper
ingredient_names: water|rice|soy sauce|brown sugar|cornstarch|ginger|garlic|red pepper flakes|chicken breast|sesame oil|broccoli|onion|carrots|water chestnuts|green bell pepper
instructions: Bring water and rice to a boil in a saucepan over high heat. Reduce heat to medium-low, cover, and simmer until rice is tender and liquid has been absorbed, about 20 to 25 minutes. Meanwhile, combine soy sauce, brown sugar, and cornstarch in a medium bowl. Stir until smooth. Stir in ginger, garlic, and red pepper flakes. Add chicken and stir to coat. Cover and marinate in the refrigerator for at least 15 minutes. Heat 1 tablespoon sesame oil in a wok or large skillet over medium-high heat. Add broccoli, onion, carrots, water chestnuts, and bell pepper. Cook and stir until just tender, about 5 minutes. Transfer vegetables to a dish and set aside. Heat remaining 1 tablespoon sesame oil in the same wok or skillet over medium-high heat. Add chicken (reserving marinade) and cook until just browned, about 2 minutes per side. Stir in vegetables and reserved marinade. Bring to a boil. Cook and stir until chicken is no longer pink in the center and vegetables are tender, about 5 to 7 minutes.
serving_suggestion: Serve over rice.

name: Chicken Marsala
description: Classic Italian-American chicken Marsala with tender chicken breasts in a rich mushroom and Marsala wine sauce.
ingredients: 100g all-purpose flour|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon black pepper|1 teaspoon dried oregano|500g chicken breast|50g butter|2 tablespoons olive oil|200g mushrooms|200ml Marsala wine|100ml cooking sherry
ingredient_names: flour|salt|black pepper|oregano|chicken breast|butter|olive oil|mushrooms|marsala wine|cooking sherry
instructions: In a shallow dish or bowl, mix together the flour, salt, pepper, and oregano. Coat chicken pieces in the flour mixture. In a large skillet, melt butter in oil over medium heat. Place chicken in the pan and lightly brown. Turn over chicken pieces and add mushrooms. Pour in wine and sherry. Cover the skillet and simmer chicken for 10 minutes, turning once, until no longer pink and juices run clear.
serving_suggestion: Serve with pasta, rice, or mashed potatoes.

name: Easy Italian Chicken
description: Simple marinated baked chicken breasts with zesty Italian dressing, baked until juicy and tender.
ingredients: 250ml Italian-style salad dressing|500g chicken breast
instructions: Pour salad dressing into a large resealable plastic bag. Add chicken breasts, seal the bag, and shake to coat. Marinate in the refrigerator for at least 1 hour or overnight. Preheat the oven to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C). Remove chicken from marinade and place in a lightly greased 9x13-inch baking dish. Discard remaining marinade. Bake in the preheated oven, turning occasionally, until cooked through and juices run clear, about 1 hour. An instant-read thermometer inserted into the center should read at least 165 degrees F (74 degrees C).
serving_suggestion: Serve with roasted vegetables or a side salad.

name: Baked Chicken Nuggets
description: Homemade baked chicken nuggets with a crispy Parmesan and herb crust, healthier than fried.
ingredients: 500g chicken breast|150g Italian seasoned bread crumbs|50g grated Parmesan cheese|1 teaspoon dried basil|1 teaspoon dried thyme|1 teaspoon salt|100g butter
instructions: Preheat the oven to 400 degrees F (200 degrees C). Cut chicken breasts into 1.5-inch pieces. Mix the bread crumbs, Parmesan cheese, basil, thyme, and salt together well in a medium bowl. Put melted butter in a bowl or dish for dipping. Dip chicken pieces into the melted butter first, then coat with the bread crumb mixture. Place the well-coated chicken nuggets on a lightly greased cookie sheet in a single layer. Bake nuggets in the preheated oven until browned and chicken is cooked through, about 20 minutes.
serving_suggestion: Serve with ketchup, honey mustard, or barbecue sauce.

name: Rolex
description: Chapati rolled with spiced scrambled eggs and fresh vegetables, a popular Ugandan street food.
ingredients: 2 chapatis|3 eggs|1 tomato|1 onion|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon black pepper|2 tablespoons cooking oil
instructions: Heat a pan with cooking oil. Whisk eggs with chopped onions, tomatoes, salt, and pepper. Pour the egg mixture into the pan and cook like an omelette. Place a chapati on top of the cooking eggs. Flip and roll tightly. Serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve as breakfast or street snack.
alternative_cooking: You can add shredded cabbage, carrots, or bell peppers for extra crunch.

name: Chapati
description: Soft, layered flatbread made from wheat flour, perfect for scooping up stews and sauces.
ingredients: 500g all-purpose flour|1 teaspoon salt|2 tablespoons sugar|3 tablespoons oil|250ml warm water
instructions: Mix flour, salt, and sugar in a bowl. Add oil and warm water gradually. Knead into a soft dough for 10 minutes. Rest for 30 minutes. Divide into balls, roll thin, fold into layers, and roll again. Cook on a hot pan until golden brown on both sides, brushing with oil.
serving_suggestion: Serve with beans, meat stew, or tea.
alternative_cooking: You can add chopped herbs like coriander to the dough.

name: Posho (Ugali)
description: Stiff maize flour porridge, a staple food across Uganda served with stews and sauces.
ingredients: 500g maize flour|1.5 litres water
instructions: Bring water to a boil in a heavy saucepan. Gradually add maize flour while stirring continuously with a wooden spoon. Reduce heat and keep stirring until mixture thickens and pulls away from the sides of the pan. Add a little more flour if needed. Cover and cook for 5 minutes. Shape into a mound and serve.
serving_suggestion: Serve with bean sauce, groundnut sauce, meat stew, or greens.
alternative_cooking: You can use cassava flour mixed with maize flour for variety.

name: Kikomando
description: Fried chapati pieces served with beans, a quick and filling Ugandan street food.
ingredients: 4 chapatis|500g cooked beans|1 onion|2 tomatoes|1 teaspoon salt|2 tablespoons cooking oil
instructions: Cut chapatis into small cubes. In a pan, heat oil and fry onions until golden. Add tomatoes and cook until soft. Add cooked beans and salt. Simmer for 5 minutes. Serve the bean mixture over the chapati cubes.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a quick lunch or dinner.
alternative_cooking: You can add fried eggs or avocado on top.

name: Muchomo
description: Grilled meat (goat, beef, or chicken) marinated with salt and spices, served with fresh salad.
ingredients: 500g goat or beef meat|2 teaspoons salt|1 teaspoon black pepper|1 teaspoon curry powder|2 tablespoons cooking oil|1 onion|2 tomatoes
instructions: Cut meat into bite-sized pieces. Marinate with salt, pepper, curry powder, and oil for 2 hours. Thread onto skewers. Grill over hot charcoal or in an oven until browned and cooked through, turning occasionally. Serve with fresh onion and tomato slices.
serving_suggestion: Serve with fresh salad, chapati, or posho.
alternative_cooking: You can add barbecue sauce or chili sauce for extra flavor.

name: Gonja
description: Roasted or fried ripe plantains, a sweet and filling street snack enjoyed across Uganda.
ingredients: 4 ripe plantains (yellow with black spots)|500ml cooking oil (for frying)|1 teaspoon salt (optional)
instructions: Method 1 - Roasting: Do not peel the plantains. Place whole unpeeled ripe plantains directly on hot charcoal or over a gas flame. Turn frequently using tongs until the skin is charred and the inside is soft and caramelized (about 10-15 minutes). Peel and serve hot. Method 2 - Frying: Peel the ripe plantains. Slice into 1cm thick rounds or cut lengthwise into strips. Heat oil in a deep pan until hot. Fry plantain pieces until golden brown on both sides (about 3-4 minutes per side). Remove and drain on paper towel. Sprinkle with salt if desired. Serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a snack with roasted groundnuts or a cup of tea.
alternative_cooking: You can also boil unpeeled plantains in water for 20-30 minutes for a softer, steamed version.

name: Mandazi
description: East African fluffy fried doughnut, slightly sweet and spiced with coconut and cardamom, perfect with tea.
ingredients: 500g all-purpose flour|100g granulated sugar|1 teaspoon active dry yeast|1 teaspoon cardamom powder|100ml coconut milk|100ml warm water|1 large egg|Oil for deep frying (about 1 litre)
instructions: Step 1: In a small bowl, dissolve yeast in warm water with 1 teaspoon sugar. Let sit for 5-10 minutes until frothy. Step 2: In a large bowl, mix flour, remaining sugar, and cardamom powder. Step 3: Add coconut milk, beaten egg, and the activated yeast mixture. Knead into a soft, smooth dough for about 10 minutes. Step 4: Cover the dough with a damp cloth and let it rest in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size. Step 5: Punch down the dough and roll it out on a floured surface to about 1cm thickness. Step 6: Cut into triangles, squares, or circles using a knife or cookie cutter. Step 7: Heat oil in a deep pot to 180°C (350°F). Step 8: Fry mandazi in batches, turning once, until golden brown on both sides (about 2-3 minutes total). Step 9: Drain on paper towel. Serve warm or at room temperature.
serving_suggestion: Serve with chai tea or coffee for breakfast or an afternoon snack.
alternative_cooking: You can add 1/2 cup grated fresh coconut to the dough for extra texture and flavor. For chocolate mandazi, add 2 tablespoons cocoa powder.

name: Pilau Rice
description: Aromatic spiced rice dish cooked with meat or vegetables, popular for weddings, parties, and special occasions.
ingredients: 500g basmati or long grain rice|300g beef or chicken, cut into bite-sized pieces|2 large onions, thinly sliced|4 cloves garlic, minced|1 tablespoon fresh ginger, grated|2 ripe tomatoes, blended or finely chopped|3 tablespoons pilau masala spice mix|500ml beef or chicken broth|4 tablespoons vegetable oil|Salt to taste
instructions: Step 1: Wash the rice thoroughly in cold water until water runs clear. Soak in water for 20 minutes, then drain. Step 2: Heat oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot (sufuria) over medium heat. Step 3: Add sliced onions and fry until golden brown and caramelized (about 8-10 minutes). Step 4: Add minced garlic and grated ginger. Fry for 1 minute until fragrant. Step 5: Add meat pieces and cook until browned on all sides (about 5-7 minutes). Step 6: Add blended tomatoes and pilau masala. Cook for 5 minutes until tomatoes reduce and oil separates. Step 7: Add the drained rice and stir gently to coat with the mixture for 2 minutes. Step 8: Pour in hot broth and add salt. Stir once, then bring to a boil. Step 9: Reduce heat to low, cover tightly with a lid, and simmer for 20 minutes without opening the lid. Step 10: Turn off the heat and let it rest covered for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork before serving.
serving_suggestion: Serve hot with kachumbari (fresh tomato, onion, and coriander salad) or plain yogurt raita.
alternative_cooking: For a vegetarian version, omit meat and use vegetable broth. Add carrots, peas, and potatoes for extra vegetables.

name: Eshabwe
description: Traditional Ankole ghee sauce made from aged butter and rock salt, served with millet bread or roasted meat. Also known as "the salt of Ankole."
ingredients: 500g aged ghee (traditional eshabwe butter, or substitute with clarified butter)|2 tablespoons rock salt (omushereka), crushed|100ml warm water (approximately)
instructions: Step 1: If using regular ghee, age it at room temperature for 2-3 days to develop the traditional tangy flavor (optional but recommended). Step 2: Place the aged ghee in a large ceramic or wooden bowl. Step 3: Add the crushed rock salt to the ghee. Step 4: Gradually add warm water, one tablespoon at a time, while whisking continuously and vigorously using a wooden whisk or fork. Step 5: Continue whisking for 10-15 minutes until the mixture emulsifies and becomes creamy, light yellow, and frothy. The consistency should be like thick cream. Step 6: Taste and adjust salt if needed. Add more warm water if too thick. Step 7: Serve immediately at room temperature. Do not refrigerate before serving as it will harden.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a dipping sauce for millet bread (oburo), roasted goat meat, steamed sweet potatoes, or boiled Irish potatoes.
alternative_cooking: You can add a pinch of baking soda (1/8 teaspoon) to speed up the emulsification process. Some families add ground peanuts for extra richness.

name: Beef Luwombo
description: Traditional Baganda dish of beef slow-cooked in banana leaves with tomatoes, onions, and spices. The banana leaf steaming method infuses the meat with an earthy, aromatic flavor.
ingredients: 500g beef (chuck or stewing beef), cut into 3cm chunks|2 ripe tomatoes, chopped|1 large onion, finely chopped|3 cloves garlic, minced|1 teaspoon salt|1 teaspoon black pepper|2 tablespoons peanut butter (optional, for thickening)|4 large fresh banana leaves|Banana fibers (ebiyayi) or kitchen string for tying
instructions: Step 1: Prepare banana leaves by passing each leaf over an open flame or hot charcoal for a few seconds until they become shiny and pliable. This prevents tearing. Wipe clean with a damp cloth. Step 2: In a bowl, combine beef chunks, chopped tomatoes, chopped onion, minced garlic, salt, black pepper, and peanut butter (if using). Mix well with your hands. Step 3: Place a banana leaf on a flat surface. Place another leaf crosswise on top to form a double layer. Step 4: Spoon the beef mixture into the center of the leaves. Step 5: Fold the leaves over the meat to form a tight parcel. Start by folding the bottom edge up, then the sides, then the top edge down. Step 6: Secure the parcel with banana fibers or kitchen string, tying it like a gift package. Ensure no gaps for steam to escape. Step 7: Place the Luwombo parcels in a large saucepan. Add about 5cm of water to the bottom of the pan (do not submerge the parcels). Step 8: Cover the saucepan tightly with a lid. You can also place extra banana leaves over the parcels before covering. Step 9: Bring water to a boil, then reduce heat to low. Steam for 2.5 to 3 hours. Check occasionally and add more water if needed. Step 10: Remove from heat. Carefully open the banana leaves (hot steam will escape). The meat should be very tender. Serve directly from the banana leaf parcel.
serving_suggestion: Serve hot with steamed matooke (green bananas), white rice, or posho (ugali).
alternative_cooking: You can also cook chicken, goat meat, or even beans using the same Luwombo method. For chicken, reduce steaming time to 1.5 hours.

name: Jollof Rice
description: Iconic West African one-pot rice dish cooked in a flavorful tomato and pepper sauce with spices and chicken or beef.
ingredients: 500g long grain parboiled rice|400g chicken or beef (optional)|3 large ripe tomatoes (blended)|1 red bell pepper (blended)|1 onion (sliced)|2 scotch bonnet peppers (to taste)|4 cloves garlic (minced)|1 tablespoon ginger paste|3 tablespoons tomato paste|2 teaspoons thyme|2 teaspoons curry powder|2 bay leaves|3 tablespoons vegetable oil|500ml chicken stock or water|Salt to taste|1 onion for frying|3 Maggi cubes (optional)
ingredient_names: rice|meat|tomatoes|bell pepper|onion|scotch bonnet|garlic|ginger|tomato paste|thyme|curry powder|bay leaves|oil|stock|salt|Maggi cubes
instructions: Step 1: Wash and parboil the rice for 10 minutes, then drain and rinse with cold water. Set aside. Step 2: Blend tomatoes, red bell pepper, and scotch bonnet peppers into a smooth puree. Step 3: Heat oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add sliced onions and fry until golden brown (about 5 minutes). Step 4: Add minced garlic and ginger paste. Fry for 1 minute until fragrant. Step 5: Add tomato paste and fry for 2 minutes until it darkens slightly. Step 6: Pour in the blended tomato-pepper mixture. Add thyme, curry powder, bay leaves, and Maggi cubes (if using). Cook for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce reduces and oil begins to separate from the tomato mixture. Step 7: If using meat, add it now and cook for 5 minutes to coat with the sauce. Step 8: Pour in the chicken stock or water. Bring to a boil. Step 9: Add salt to taste. The sauce should be slightly over-salted as the rice will absorb it. Step 10: Add the parboiled rice and stir gently to combine. Step 11: Reduce heat to low, cover tightly with a lid (seal with foil if lid doesn't fit tightly). Step 12: Cook for 25-30 minutes without opening the lid. Step 13: After 25 minutes, check the rice. If not fully cooked, add 2 tablespoons of water, cover, and cook for another 5-10 minutes. Step 14: Fluff the rice with a fork. Serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve with fried plantains (dodo), coleslaw, or a side of fresh tomato and onion salad.
alternative_cooking: For a vegetarian version, omit the meat and use vegetable stock. Add fried tofu or vegetables like carrots, peas, and green beans.

name: Fufu
description: Starchy, doughy African staple made from cassava and plantains or yams, served as an accompaniment to soups and stews.
ingredients: 1kg cassava (or frozen cassava)|2 green plantains (or 500g yams)|Water for boiling
instructions: Method 1 - Traditional (Cassava and Plantain): Step 1: Peel the cassava and remove the woody core. Cut into chunks. Step 2: Peel the green plantains and cut into chunks. Step 3: Boil cassava and plantain chunks together in salted water for 25-30 minutes until very soft and fork-tender. Step 4: Drain the water, reserving about 1 cup. Step 5: While still hot, transfer the cooked cassava and plantains to a large mortar (or heavy bowl). Step 6: Pound vigorously with a wooden pestle (or use a stand mixer with a dough hook) until smooth, elastic, and lump-free. Add reserved water a little at a time to achieve a soft, pliable dough. Step 7: Continue pounding for 10-15 minutes until the fufu is smooth and stretchy. Step 8: Shape into a ball or individual serving portions. Method 2 - Using Fufu Flour: Step 1: Bring 4 cups of water to a boil in a pot. Step 2: In a bowl, mix 2 cups of fufu flour with 1 cup of cold water to form a smooth paste. Step 3: Pour the paste into the boiling water while stirring continuously with a wooden spoon. Step 4: Reduce heat and keep stirring until the mixture thickens into a smooth, stretchy dough (about 5-8 minutes). Step 5: Add a little more hot water if too thick. Step 6: Cover and cook for 2 minutes, then stir again. Step 7: Shape into balls. To serve: Wet your hands with water to prevent sticking. Pinch off a small piece of fufu, roll into a ball, and make an indentation with your thumb. Use this to scoop up soup or stew.
serving_suggestion: Serve with egusi soup, groundnut soup, okra soup, or any African stew.
alternative_cooking: You can make fufu using only cassava (akpu), only plantains (plantain fufu), or yams (yam fufu). Store leftover fufu wrapped in plastic wrap in the refrigerator; reheat by steaming.

name: Doro Wat
description: Ethiopian national dish of chicken slow-cooked in a deeply spiced red onion and berbere sauce, served with hard-boiled eggs.
ingredients: 1 whole chicken (cut into 12 pieces)|4 large red onions (finely chopped)|6 cloves garlic (minced)|2 tablespoons ginger (grated)|4 tablespoons berbere spice mix|2 tablespoons paprika|1 tablespoon tomato paste|1 cup clarified butter (niter kibbeh) or regular butter|4 hard-boiled eggs|500ml water or chicken stock|Salt to taste|1 tablespoon lemon juice|2 teaspoons cardamom powder
instructions: Step 1: Prepare niter kibbeh (Ethiopian spiced clarified butter): Heat butter with cardamom, cinnamon, and other spices for 15 minutes, then strain (optional but recommended). Step 2: In a large heavy-bottomed pot (clay pot is traditional), heat the niter kibbeh over medium-low heat. Step 3: Add chopped red onions and cook very slowly for 30-45 minutes, stirring frequently, until the onions are soft, dark brown, and caramelized. Do not rush this step. Step 4: Add minced garlic and grated ginger. Cook for 2 minutes until fragrant. Step 5: Add berbere spice mix and paprika. Stir and cook for 3-4 minutes until the spices are aromatic and darken slightly. Step 6: Add tomato paste and cook for 2 more minutes. Step 7: Add the chicken pieces and stir to coat with the onion-spice mixture. Step 8: Add water or chicken stock, salt, and lemon juice. Bring to a boil. Step 9: Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. Step 10: Uncover and continue cooking for another 15-20 minutes until the sauce thickens and oil rises to the top. Step 11: Gently add the hard-boiled eggs (whole or halved) into the sauce. Step 12: Simmer for 5 more minutes to allow the eggs to absorb the flavors. Step 13: Taste and adjust salt and spice level. The sauce should be thick, dark red, and very flavorful. Serve hot.
serving_suggestion: Serve with injera (Ethiopian sourdough flatbread) or rice. Traditionally eaten with hands, using injera to scoop up the chicken, egg, and sauce.
alternative_cooking: For a vegetarian version, substitute chicken with 500g of mushrooms or chickpeas. Reduce cooking time to 30 minutes.

name: Suya
description: Nigerian spicy grilled meat skewers coated in ground peanut and chili spice blend (yaji), served with fresh onions and tomatoes.
ingredients: 500g beef (sirloin or flank steak) cut into thin strips|100g ground roasted peanuts (peanut powder)|2 tablespoons ground cayenne pepper|1 tablespoon ground ginger|1 tablespoon garlic powder|1 teaspoon smoked paprika|1 teaspoon salt|1 Maggi cube (crushed)|2 tablespoons vegetable oil|1 large onion (sliced)|2 tomatoes (sliced)
instructions: Step 1 - Make the Yaji spice mix: In a dry bowl, combine ground peanuts, cayenne pepper, ground ginger, garlic powder, smoked paprika, salt, and crushed Maggi cube. Mix well. Set aside 1/4 cup for serving. Step 2 - Marinate the meat: Place beef strips in a large bowl. Add the remaining yaji spice mix and vegetable oil. Massage the spices into the meat until fully coated. Step 3 - Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight for deeper flavor. Step 4 - Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes (to prevent burning). Step 5 - Thread the marinated beef strips onto the skewers, weaving them back and forth. Do not overcrowd; leave some space for even cooking. Step 6 - Preheat a grill (charcoal is traditional) or a grill pan over medium-high heat. Step 7 - Grill the suya skewers for 3-4 minutes per side until charred on the edges and cooked through. Step 8 - Remove from grill and immediately roll the hot skewers in the reserved yaji spice mix. Step 9 - Serve hot, topped with sliced onions and tomatoes. Squeeze fresh lemon juice over if desired.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a street snack or appetizer with extra sliced onions, tomatoes, cucumber, and fresh lemon wedges.
alternative_cooking: You can also make suya with chicken, goat meat, or even tofu. For chicken suya, reduce grilling time to 2-3 minutes per side.

name: Bobotie
description: South African baked dish of spiced minced meat with a creamy egg topping, sweet and savory with curry and dried fruit.
ingredients: 500g ground beef or lamb|2 slices white bread (crusts removed)|250ml milk|2 onions (finely chopped)|2 cloves garlic (minced)|2 tablespoons curry powder|1 teaspoon turmeric|1 teaspoon ground cumin|1 teaspoon ground coriander|1 tablespoon apricot jam|50g raisins or sultanas|50g slivered almonds (optional)|2 tablespoons chutney (Mrs. Balls is traditional)|2 tablespoons lemon juice|2 eggs (for topping)|Salt and black pepper to taste|2 bay leaves
ingredient_names: ground beef|bread|milk|onions|garlic|curry powder|turmeric|cumin|coriander|apricot jam|raisins|almonds|chutney|lemon juice|eggs|salt|pepper|bay leaves
instructions: Step 1 - Soak the bread: Place the bread in a small bowl and pour 150ml of milk over it. Let it soak for 5 minutes. Step 2 - Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Step 3 - Fry the aromatics: Heat oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Add chopped onions and cook until soft and translucent (about 5 minutes). Add garlic and cook for 1 minute. Step 4 - Add spices: Stir in curry powder, turmeric, cumin, and coriander. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Step 5 - Cook the meat: Add ground beef and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, until browned (about 8-10 minutes). Step 6 - Add sweet and savory ingredients: Stir in apricot jam, raisins, almonds (if using), chutney, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Step 7 - Squeeze the soaked bread to remove excess milk. Crumble the bread into the meat mixture. Stir to combine. Step 8 - Transfer the meat mixture to a greased baking dish (about 20x20cm or similar). Press down gently and smooth the top. Step 9 - Make the egg topping: In a bowl, whisk the remaining 100ml milk with 2 eggs and a pinch of salt and turmeric. Step 10 - Pour the egg mixture over the meat mixture. Place bay leaves on top. Step 11 - Bake for 30-35 minutes until the topping is set, golden brown, and slightly puffed. Step 12 - Let it rest for 5-10 minutes before serving.
serving_suggestion: Serve hot with yellow rice (rice cooked with turmeric and raisins), chutney, and banana slices.
alternative_cooking: You can add 2 tablespoons of grated apple or grated carrot to the meat mixture for extra moisture and sweetness. For a lighter version, use half beef and half lamb.

name: Chakalaka
description: Spicy South African vegetable relish made with beans, carrots, peppers, and curry spices, often served with bread or pap.
ingredients: 2 carrots (grated)|1 green bell pepper (finely chopped)|1 red bell pepper (finely chopped)|1 onion (finely chopped)|2 tomatoes (chopped)|1 can baked beans (400g) or sugar beans|2 cloves garlic (minced)|1 tablespoon fresh ginger (grated)|2 tablespoons curry powder|1 teaspoon paprika|1 teaspoon cayenne pepper (optional)|2 tablespoons vegetable oil|Salt and black pepper to taste|Fresh coriander for garnish (optional)
instructions: Step 1 - Prep all vegetables: Grate carrots, chop bell peppers and onion, mince garlic, and grate ginger. Step 2 - Heat oil in a large skillet or pot over medium heat. Step 3 - Add onion and cook until soft and translucent (about 3-4 minutes). Step 4 - Add garlic and ginger. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Step 5 - Add grated carrots and chopped bell peppers. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until slightly softened. Step 6 - Add curry powder, paprika, and cayenne (if using). Stir and cook for 1 minute to toast the spices. Step 7 - Add chopped tomatoes and cook for 3-4 minutes until they break down. Step 8 - Add the baked beans (with their sauce) or drained sugar beans. Stir well to combine. Step 9 - Reduce heat to low and simmer for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until vegetables are tender and flavors have melded. Step 10 - Add salt and black pepper to taste. Step 11 - Garnish with fresh coriander if desired. Serve warm or at room temperature.
serving_suggestion: Serve as a side dish with pap (maize porridge), bread, grilled meat (braai), or as a topping for hot dogs and burgers.
alternative_cooking: You can add cabbage, zucchini, or butternut squash for extra vegetables. Chakalaka tastes even better the next day after flavors develop. Store in refrigerator for up to 5 days.

name: Sadza
description: Zimbabwean stiff maize meal porridge, similar to posho/ugali, the staple starch eaten with stews and vegetables.
ingredients: 500g white maize meal (sadza flour or any finely ground cornmeal)|1.5 litres water|1 teaspoon salt (optional)
instructions: Step 1 - Bring water to a boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add salt if desired. Step 2 - Gradually add 1 cup of maize meal to the boiling water while stirring continuously with a wooden spoon or traditional wooden paddle (mutwi). Step 3 - Reduce heat to medium. Keep stirring to prevent lumps from forming. The mixture will thicken into a porridge consistency. Step 4 - Cover and cook for 5 minutes. Step 5 - Add the remaining maize meal gradually, a handful at a time, while stirring vigorously. Step 6 - Continue stirring and pressing the mixture against the side of the pot to ensure even cooking. Step 7 - When the sadza becomes thick and starts pulling away from the sides of the pot, reduce heat to low. Step 8 - Cover and let it cook for 5-10 minutes. Do not stir during this time. Step 9 - Uncover and stir again vigorously with the paddle, folding the sadza over itself to incorporate any remaining uncooked flour. Step 10 - Test for doneness: The sadza should be firm, smooth, and not sticky. If too soft, add a little more maize meal and stir. If too stiff, add a splash of hot water. Step 11 - Cover and cook for another 5 minutes. Step 12 - Turn the sadza out onto a plate or serving board. Traditionally, it is flipped out of the pot onto a flat surface. To serve: Wet your hands with water. Scoop a portion of sadza, roll it into a ball, and make an indentation with your thumb. Use this to scoop up stew or sauce.
serving_suggestion: Serve hot with meat stew, chicken stew, bean sauce, collard greens (muriwo), or any vegetable relish.
alternative_cooking: You can use a stand mixer with a dough hook to make sadza if you don't have a wooden paddle. The texture should be firmer than mashed potatoes but softer than bread dough.

name: Nyama Choma
description: East African grilled meat (usually goat or beef), slow-roasted over charcoal until crispy outside and tender inside.
ingredients: 1 kg goat meat or beef (ribs or leg, with bone for flavor)|2 tablespoons salt|1 tablespoon black pepper|1 tablespoon garlic powder|1 tablespoon ginger powder|2 teaspoons cayenne pepper (optional)|1 tablespoon curry powder|2 tablespoons vegetable oil|1 lemon (cut into wedges)|For serving: 1 onion (sliced), 2 tomatoes (sliced), 1 chili pepper (chopped)
instructions: Step 1 - Prepare the meat: Wash the meat and pat dry with paper towels. Cut into large chunks or leave ribs whole. Step 2 - Make the marinade: In a small bowl, mix salt, black pepper, garlic powder, ginger powder, cayenne pepper (if using), curry powder, and vegetable oil to form a paste. Step 3 - Rub the marinade generously all over the meat, ensuring every piece is coated. Step 4 - Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight for best flavor. Step 5 - Prepare the charcoal grill (traditional) or gas grill. The fire should be medium-hot with glowing coals (not flaming). Step 6 - Remove meat from refrigerator 30 minutes before grilling to come to room temperature. Step 7 - Place meat on the grill, bone side down if applicable. Step 8 - Grill for 30-40 minutes, turning every 10 minutes, until the outside is charred and crispy and the inside is cooked through but still juicy. Step 9 - For well-done meat (traditional preference), cook until the meat pulls away from the bone easily, about 45-60 minutes total. Step 10 - Check doneness by cutting into a thick piece. The juices should run clear. Step 11 - Remove from grill and let rest for 10 minutes before serving. Step 12 - Cut into smaller serving pieces if desired. To serve: Squeeze fresh lemon juice over the meat. Serve with sliced onions, tomatoes, and chili pepper on the side.
serving_suggestion: Serve with kachumbari (fresh tomato and onion salad), ugali/posho, or chapati. Cold beer or soda is the traditional pairing.
alternative_cooking: You can also roast nyama choma in an oven: Preheat to 200°C (400°F), place meat on a rack over a baking pan, and roast for 45-60 minutes. Chicken nyama choma is also popular; reduce cooking time to 30 minutes.

name: Spaghetti Bolognese
description: Classic Italian pasta dish with a slow-cooked meat sauce made from beef, tomatoes, carrots, celery, and red wine.
ingredients: 400g spaghetti pasta|500g ground beef (or half beef, half pork)|2 tablespoons olive oil|1 onion (finely chopped)|2 carrots (finely chopped)|2 celery stalks (finely chopped)|3 cloves garlic (minced)|800g crushed tomatoes|100ml red wine (optional)|100ml milk|2 tablespoons tomato paste|1 bay leaf|1 teaspoon dried oregano|Salt and black pepper to taste|Fresh basil for garnish|Grated Parmesan cheese for serving
ingredient_names: spaghetti|ground beef|olive oil|onion|carrots|celery|garlic|crushed tomatoes|red wine|milk|tomato paste|bay leaf|oregano|salt|pepper|basil|Parmesan
instructions: Step 1 - Start the sauce (Bolognese requires slow cooking): Heat olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Step 2 - Add onion, carrots, and celery (this is called soffritto). Cook for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until vegetables are soft and onion is translucent. Step 3 - Add minced garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Step 4 - Add ground beef (and pork if using). Break up the meat with a wooden spoon and cook until browned all over (about 8-10 minutes). Step 5 - Pour in red wine (if using). Scrape the bottom of the pot to release any browned bits. Let the wine bubble and reduce for 2-3 minutes until mostly evaporated. Step 6 - Stir in tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes to deepen the flavor. Step 7 - Add crushed tomatoes, bay leaf, oregano, salt, and pepper. Stir to combine. Step 8 - Bring the sauce to a simmer, then reduce heat to low. Cover partially and cook for 1.5 to 2 hours, stirring occasionally. The longer it cooks, the better the flavor. Step 9 - After 1.5 hours, stir in the milk. This adds richness and balances the acidity of the tomatoes. Continue cooking for another 30 minutes. Step 10 - While sauce is cooking, bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Step 11 - Cook spaghetti according to package instructions until al dente (firm to the bite). Step 12 - Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining. Step 13 - Drain spaghetti and return to the pot. Step 14 - Add a few ladles of Bolognese sauce to the spaghetti. Toss to coat, adding a little pasta water if needed to loosen the sauce. Step 15 - Remove bay leaf from remaining sauce. Step 16 - Serve spaghetti on plates or in bowls. Top with more Bolognese sauce. Step 17 - Garnish with fresh basil and generous grated Parmesan cheese.
serving_suggestion: Serve hot with extra grated Parmesan cheese on the side and a green salad with garlic bread.
alternative_cooking: For a richer sauce, use a mix of ground beef, pork, and pancetta. For a lighter version, use ground turkey and skip the milk. Leftover sauce freezes well for up to 3 months.

name: Carbonara
description: Roman pasta dish made with eggs, hard cheese, cured pork (guanciale or pancetta), and black pepper — no cream.
ingredients: 400g spaghetti or rigatoni|150g guanciale or pancetta (diced)|3 large eggs|1 large egg yolk|100g Pecorino Romano cheese (grated)|50g Parmesan cheese (grated)|Freshly ground black pepper|Salt for pasta water
instructions: Step 1 - Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Use about 1 tablespoon of salt per 4 litres of water. Step 2 - While water is heating, prepare the other ingredients. Step 3 - In a large bowl, whisk together the 3 whole eggs, 1 egg yolk, grated Pecorino Romano, grated Parmesan, and a generous amount of freshly ground black pepper. Whisk until smooth and well combined. Do this while the pasta is cooking. Step 4 - Heat a large skillet over medium heat. Add diced guanciale or pancetta. Step 5 - Cook until the pork is crispy and golden brown, about 5-7 minutes. The fat should render out. Do not drain the fat — it adds flavor. Step 6 - Turn off the heat on the skillet and set aside. Step 7 - Add spaghetti to the boiling water and cook according to package instructions until al dente. Step 8 - Just before draining, reserve 1 cup of the starchy pasta water. Step 9 - Drain the spaghetti (do not rinse). Step 10 - Add the hot drained spaghetti directly into the skillet with the crispy guanciale and rendered fat. Toss to coat the pasta in the fat. Step 11 - Remove the skillet from the heat completely. This is crucial — if the pan is too hot, the eggs will scramble. Step 12 - Pour the egg and cheese mixture over the pasta. Toss quickly and vigorously with tongs to coat every strand. The residual heat from the pasta will cook the eggs gently, creating a creamy sauce. Step 13 - Add the reserved pasta water, a little at a time (about 1/4 cup), while tossing until the sauce becomes silky and coats the pasta beautifully. Do not add too much water. Step 14 - Taste and add more black pepper if needed. The cheese is salty, so additional salt is rarely needed. Step 15 - Serve immediately on warm plates. Carbonara waits for no one — it must be served right away while creamy. Step 16 - Top with extra grated cheese and more black pepper.
serving_suggestion: Serve immediately with a crisp green salad and crusty bread. Never refrigerate and reheat carbonara as the eggs will scramble.
alternative_cooking: Traditional Carbonara uses guanciale (cured pork cheek) and Pecorino Romano. Pancetta and Parmesan are acceptable substitutes but change the flavor. Never add cream — authentic Carbonara has no cream.

name: Mac and Cheese
description: Creamy baked macaroni and cheese with a golden, crispy topping — classic American comfort food.
ingredients: 400g elbow macaroni|50g butter|50g all-purpose flour|750ml whole milk (warm)|250g sharp cheddar cheese (grated)|100g Gruyčre or mozzarella cheese (grated)|1 teaspoon salt|1/2 teaspoon black pepper|1/2 teaspoon paprika|1/4 teaspoon nutmeg (optional)|50g breadcrumbs (panko or regular)|2 tablespoons Parmesan cheese (grated)|2 tablespoons melted butter (for topping)
instructions: Step 1 - Preheat oven to 190°C (375°F). Grease a 9x13 inch baking dish. Step 2 - Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Step 3 - Cook macaroni according to package instructions until al dente (about 1 minute less than package says, as it will cook more in the oven). Step 4 - Drain pasta and set aside. Do not rinse. Step 5 - Make the cheese sauce (béchamel): In a large saucepan, melt 50g butter over medium heat. Step 6 - Add flour and whisk continuously for 1-2 minutes until the mixture is bubbly and pale golden (this is a roux).
